Motion For Good Faith Settlement Determination And 2 Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof (Motions)
Off Calendar Apr-15-2022 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF PURPLE SURGICAL UK LIMITED's Motion For Good Faith Settlement Determination.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion for determination of good faith settlement is granted.

Wong's agreement to pay $3,925,000.00 to settle is "in the ballpark" and in good faith within the meaning of Tech-Bilt, Inc. v. Woodward-Clyde & Assocs. (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488, 499 [holding that the court should examine the following factors to determine whether a settlement is in good faith: (1) a rough approximation of plaintiffs' total recovery and the settlor's proportionate liability; (2) the amount paid in settlement; (3) the allocation of settlement proceeds among plaintiffs; (4) a recognition that a settlor should pay less in settlement than he would if he were found liable after a trial; (5) the financial conditions of the settling defendants, as well as the existence of collusion, fraud, or tortious conduct aimed to injure the interests of non-settling defendants; and (6) defendant's settlement figure must not be grossly disproportionate to what a reasonable person, at the time of the settlement, would estimate the settling defendant's liability to be.].)

First, Win Billion Investment Group Ltd. and Wu Ric ("WB") are the only parties to respond to this motion and they do not contest that the settlement was made in good faith. The Tech-Bilt factors become relevant and must be considered "only when the good faith nature of a settlement is disputed . . . . That is to say, when no one objects, the barebones motion which sets forth the ground of good faith, accompanied by a declaration which sets forth a brief background of the case is sufficient." (City of Grand Terrace v. Superior Court (1987) 192 Cal.App.3d 1251, 1261.)

Second, substantial evidence supports a finding of good faith. Wong is paying a substantial amount through his insurers in light of the rough approximation of plaintiff's recovery and Wong's proportionate liability and the party asserting the lack of good faith has the burden of proof on that issue. (See Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 877.6(d).) In this case, there is no showing of collusion or bad faith.

The preclusive effect of this settlement on WB's cross-complaint against Wong under CCP 877.6(c) is not properly before the court at this time. WB and/or Wong may brief that issue in subsequent proceedings.

Order signed.

Judge: Suzanne R. Bolanos; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported (302/SRB)
